cross_domain_id_notes: Immigration edges reference HOU-A01 — should point to HOU-A03 (immigration as housing driver)

sources.author: Climate Change Committee

nodes.statement: The UK is missing approximately 4.3 million homes according to Centre for Cities analysis, with net additional dwellings in England at 208,600 in 2024/25 representing a 6% decline
London had just 731 private housing starts in H1 2025, and planning permissions hit record lows at 42,239 approved in Q2 2025 representing a 19% decline
Savills forecasts 840,000 completions by 2028/29, which is 42% short of the 1.5 million target for the parliament
Over 110,000 households are in temporary accommodation at record levels, costing local councils £1.7 billion per year
Government spending on housing in England fell 45% in real terms between 2009/10 and 2015/16 and remained 20% lower in 2022/23 than pre-austerity levels
Housebuilding rates dropped by more than a third after the 1947 Town and Country Planning Act, from 2% growth per year to 1.2% between 1947 and 2019
Approximately 2 million council homes have been sold under Right to Buy since 1980, with social housing stock declining from 5.5 million to approximately 4 million as only a fraction were replaced

The private sector peaked at approximately 200,000 completions per year, and 300,000 or more was only achieved in the 1950s-1970s when councils built over 100,000 homes per year alongside the private sector
Every £1 spent on housing adaptations and insulation saves between £2 and £7 in health and social care costs according to NICE evidence
The UK has the oldest and least energy-efficient housing stock in Europe, with millions of homes below EPC C driving fuel poverty and respiratory illness
The average house price to earnings ratio has roughly doubled since the 1990s, with affordability primarily a London and South East problem that has spread to other prosperous cities
Five large housebuilders control approximately 50% of private sector output and release homes at rates that maintain prices, creating structural constraints on supply
Labour announced a £39 billion Social and Affordable Homes Programme for 2026-2036 with a 60% target for social rent homes
The UK can build 300,000 homes per year under current policy settings including planning reform alone
nodes.verdict: NOT ACHIEVABLE without major public sector housebuilding alongside planning reform. Private sector peaked at 200k. 300k only achieved historically when councils built at scale. Savills forecasts 42% shortfall by 2028/29.

nodes.statement: The planning system is the main barrier to increased housebuilding in the UK
nodes.verdict: MAJOR BARRIER BUT NOT ONLY ONE. Housebuilding dropped >1/3 after 1947 Act. But demand, finance, construction capacity, and the housebuilder business model are co-constraints. Countries with rules-based zoning build more consistently.

nodes.statement: Immigration is the primary driver of the UK housing crisis
nodes.verdict: UNDERSUPPLY IS PRIMARY. 4.3m deficit existed before post-2021 migration surge. Housing spending fell 45% during austerity. Undersupply concentrated in cities where costs are highest regardless of immigration. Immigration adds to demand but is a secondary factor.

nodes.statement: Right to Buy has been beneficial for the UK housing system overall
nodes.verdict: HELPED INDIVIDUALS, DEVASTATED SOCIAL STOCK. 2m homes sold, only fraction replaced. Stock declined from 5.5m to 4m. Receipts restricted so councils couldn't build replacements. RTB needs fundamental reform with 1:1 replacement minimum.

nodes.statement: Rent controls are an effective solution to the UK's housing affordability crisis
nodes.verdict: NOT A PRIMARY SOLUTION. First-generation strict controls reduce supply and quality. Second-generation inflation-linked caps have moderate effects. UK problem is primarily supply not price regulation. Useful as short-term stabiliser alongside supply increase only.

nodes.statement: The private sector alone can deliver housing at the scale needed to close the UK's housing deficit
nodes.verdict: NO. HISTORIC EVIDENCE IS UNAMBIGUOUS. Private sector peaked at 200k/yr. 5 companies control 50% of output. The market alone will not solve a crisis where high prices are profitable for incumbents. Public sector essential.

nodes.statement: Building significantly more homes will reduce housing prices and improve affordability
nodes.verdict: YES BUT SLOWLY AND AT SCALE. Bank of England, IFS, Resolution Foundation all agree supply moderates prices but gradually. Requires consistently higher supply over decades not years. Not a quick fix.

nodes.statement: The quality and energy efficiency of the UK's existing housing stock is in crisis
nodes.verdict: YES, MEASURABLY. Oldest housing stock in Europe. Millions below EPC C. Cold homes drive fuel poverty and respiratory illness. Insulation is most cost-effective carbon abatement at negative cost over building lifetime. Quality is as important as quantity.

nodes.statement: Every party promising 1.5 million or more homes without explaining how councils will build 50,000 or more per year is not being honest because the private sector has never delivered 300,000 alone
The UK has underbuilt homes for 80 years and government spending cuts during austerity are the primary driver of the crisis, not immigration or any single policy failure
Retrofit of existing housing stock is the highest return-on-investment housing policy available, with every £1 on insulation saving £2-7 in health costs and more than £1 in energy costs
Land value capture reform would fund affordable housing from the 10-100x increase in land values that planning permission creates, but no party has proposed it at the required scale

Revive council housebuilding to 50,000 or more homes per year alongside housing associations and private sector, funded through the £39bn SAHP with 60% social rent and supported by a National Housing Bank providing low-cost long-term finance

Replace the discretionary planning system with rules-based zoning over 10 years, where local plans define what can be built and conforming proposals get automatic permission, following the Japanese and German models
Reform compulsory purchase to enable land acquisition at closer to existing use value, capturing the 10-100x planning uplift for public benefit including infrastructure and affordable homes
Deliver a national retrofit programme insulating 1 million homes per year through a National Insulation Service, with EPC C minimum for all rented properties by 2028
Reform Right to Buy with a mandatory 1:1 replacement requirement nationally and give local authorities the power to suspend the scheme in areas of acute housing shortage
Require 20-30% of publicly funded new homes to use Modern Methods of Construction including prefabricated and modular building to reduce build times by 30-50% and costs by 10-20%
Introduce rent stabilisation for sitting tenants via an inflation-linked cap as an interim measure while supply increases, avoiding strict first-generation controls that reduce supply
Build 1.6 million homes over the parliament without mandatory local targets, prioritise brownfield development, extend Help to Buy and Mortgage Guarantee Scheme, and maintain Right to Buy

Build 1.5 million homes with restored mandatory local targets, planning reform via NPPF update, New Towns programme, release grey belt land with 50% affordable, and deliver the £39bn Social and Affordable Homes Programme
Build 380,000 homes per year including 150,000 social homes, create ten new garden cities, abolish residential leasehold, end Right to Buy locally, and restore EPC C minimum for landlords
Build 150,000 social homes per year, invest £29bn over 5 years to raise all homes to EPC B, introduce rent controls, and allow councils to requisition empty or poorly insulated properties

Fast-track brownfield planning with tax incentives, prioritise local people for social housing with foreign nationals moved to the back of the queue, scrap the Renters Reform Bill, and state no housing targets
